I am not sure if this is a proper fix, because

```
depends: R_library('ggplot2')
task:
R:
    library(ggplot2)
```

is difficult to interpret. When the script is executed, `depends` will be processed so local machine should have this library. Then, during execution, the task might be sent to a remote server where `R_library` is also copied. With the new patch, the same `R_library` will be checked.

So if we are treating other targets as the same as file targets and require the targets exist in both local and remote systems, this behavior makes sense. If we are to support remote targets (unavailable locally, #475 ), we will have to do something to these non-files targets as well.

The global section is designed to contain "definitions" that will be used by each step, and is executed at the beginning of each step in the current implementation. Although it is possible to define a `sos_dict` that contains the global definitions (after executing global section once) and be used by each step, the dictionary might not be pickleable and thus cannot be passed to each worker.

So basically the global section should only contain definitions, not any computation.

@AdriaanRol (and everyone else)

On DataSets containing DataSets: To me, this doesn't really fit my conceptual model of what a data set is, but that is probably because my mental model of an experiment is not accurate.

My (simplistic) mental model is that you do some basic set-up, then you run your experiment by setting a few parameters to different values and measuring a few variables at the different settings.

From your example, it sounds like this is incorrect, and that a more accurate description of an experiment would allow for different stages to occur, where each stage is a sweep more or less as I've described (potentially adaptive, if it's a calibration sweep, but that's not conceptually a problem), but where you may perform a particular stage (e.g., calibration) more than once, and indeed you might want to interrupt one sweep (the main measurement sweep) and interject another sweep.

I fear that trying to glue together DataSets within DataSets to handle all of these possibilities will turn into a nightmare of complexity. I'd be much more inclined to use references (by GUID or filename or whatever) from one DataSet to another. 

One somewhat related question: it sounds like the values in each result may be different for different sweep points; e.g., you might only do certain computations every N points, but you want to store the computed values in the DataSet. This was my thinking behind optional parameters: values that were in some results but not all. In the latest version I've dropped "optional" and effectively all parameters are optional. Does this give you enough flexibility?

I got around this quite easily by downloading with Multi30k.download(DATAROOT) and then just using TranslationDataset.splits instead of Multi30k.splits. Pass the rootpath to the path argument instead of the root argument


```

import spacy
from torchtext.datasets import TranslationDataset, Multi30k
from torchtext.data import BucketIterator, interleave_keys, Field 
from onmt.inputters import OrderedIterator

DATAROOT = '~/Python/DATASETS/Multi30k/'
Multi30k.download(DATAROOT)






def  prep_torchtext_multi30k( 
                          dataroot = '~/Python/DATASETS/Multi30k/',
                          maxwords = 12000, 
                          bsize =32, 
                          langs = ['de','en'],
                          exts =  ['.en','.de'],
                          ):

    # modifies dataset loader from https://github.com/A-Jacobson/minimal-nmt
    
    
    try:     de, en  = [ load_multi30k_torchtext.nlp.get(lang) for lang in langs]
    except:  de, en  = [ spacy.load(lang, disable=['tagger', 'parser', 'ner']) for lang in langs] 
    # avoid repeatedly reloading spacy models --> heavy mem use
    prep_torchtext_multi30k.nlp = {'en':en, 'de':de}
        
    
    def tok_src(text): return [tok.text for tok in de.tokenizer(text) if not tok.is_space]
    def tok_tgt(text): return [tok.text for tok in en.tokenizer(text) if not tok.is_space]

    SRC = Field( tokenize = tok_src, init_token='<s>',  eos_token='</s>' )
    TGT = Field( tokenize = tok_tgt, init_token='<s>',  eos_token='</s>' )
    
    trnset, valset, testset = TranslationDataset.splits(   
        
                                      path       = dataroot,  
                                      exts       = exts,   
                                      fields     = [('src', SRC), ('trg',TGT)],
                                      train      = 'train', 
                                      validation = 'val', 
                                      test       = 'test2016')

    for (nm, field) in [('src', SRC), ('trg',TGT)]:  
        trnsubset = getattr(trnset, nm) 
        field.build_vocab( trnsubset, max_size = maxwords)
    
       
    # ONMT's OrderedIterator --> subclasses BucketIterator but better at packing batches together. 
    # however also want to recreate torchtext's  interleave_keys which prevents excessive
    # padding on both src and tgt sides

    trniter, valiter, tstiter = OrderedIterator.splits(   
                                   datasets = [trnset, valset, testset], 
                                   batch_size = bsize, 
                                   sort_key = lambda ex: interleave_keys(len(ex.src), len(ex.trg),
                                   device='cuda' )

    return (trnset, valset, testset), (trniter, valiter, tstiter), (SRC.vocab, TGT.vocab)

```

```
let bucket = new aws.s3.Bucket("mybucket");
```

The `name` parameter is used for two purposes:
1. To create the URN for the resource so that we can give it an identity that can be related to resources from previous checkpoints
2. As a seed for AutoName generation of the `name` property on the resource itself

For 2, you can pass `name` explicitly via an `Output<string>`, so it's always possible to control the actual name of the cloud resource based on an output.  You cannot opt-in to auto-naming using this approach though - at least not directly.  Making that easier is tracked by https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi/issues/381.

For 1, it's dangerous to allow not knowing a name to use for resource identity until after some other resource resolves.  This would for example make the identity of the resource un-knowable during preview.  This is mostly equivalent to creating a resource inside an `apply` (and in fact, that's a way of working around this).  I'd be interested in seeing a real compelling case where this is needed.

Do you have examples of either/both of these in practice?

Right now it's a matter of convenience for when you want to spin up a ray cluster using docker-compose. Docker-compose allows you to define services and link them to each other. 

Imagine having two services, master (for ray master) and worker (for ray workers). You don't know what IP docker will be assign to the master service so using the hostname makes it easier.

You could explicitly set an IP when defining these services in the docker-compose.yml but it's just cleaner using hostnames. Docker-compose and Docker-swarm also makes it simple to scale these services. 

With that regex change, I've found that workers can use hostnames to connect to master via
"ray start --redis-address master:6379".

However, you still need to set the explicit IP when launching ray on the master node.
So you can't set --node-ip-address on master to its hostname.

Can you create a separate issue for this one?

Our serialization code is written in C++ and is normally quite fast when mapping Python objects to the Arrow format. However, the C++ code only handles primitive types and lists/dicts/tuples. So when it encounters something it doesn't know how to serialize (e.g., a set), then it falls back to using cloudpickle (so it leaves C++ and goes back to Python).

There are a couple things that could be done.
- Add a special case for sets in the C++ code (this probably makes sense, but it won't work if you replace the sets in your example with some custom Python class).
- We need to generally optimize this code path more.

That does not work: the `default` function is called *only* when the _obj_ is not an instance of a _primitive_ type (from the JSON point of view, that is).

Consider:
```python
import rapidjson

class Foo:
    pass

def default(obj):
    print("obj is a %r" % type(obj))
    if isinstance(obj, Foo):
        return {'__class__': 'Foo'}
    else:
        return obj

print('RESULT=', rapidjson.dumps(['a', 1, Foo()], default=default))
```

This emits:
```
obj is a <class '__main__.Foo'>
RESULT= ["a",1,{"__class__":"Foo"}]
```

You can see that the function was called once, only for the custom type.

Performing the call unconditionally would introduce a serious speed penality, so I'm afraid that's not going to happen. OTOH, if you could describe the use case and demonstrate its generic usefulness, then maybe a _different_ new argument could be added, or new method(s) similar to [Decoder.string()](http://python-rapidjson.readthedocs.io/en/latest/decoder.html#rapidjson.Decoder.string) could be implemented (but that would prompt for orthogonal features(s) on the `Encoder` class....).

So again, can you explain your use case?

The `DrawControl` widget owns a `layer` attribute which is a `FeatureGroup` instance, you can add new layers (rectangle, markers...) to this `layer` attribute from the python side and it works fine. 

BUT if a new layer has been added to this featuregroup from the javascript side (for instance when drawing a rectangle with your mouse), the list of layers won't be synchronized anymore between the python and javascript. So you won't have access to new added layers from the python side, which is why it's not possible with the current implementation of `FeatureGroup` to implement your request.

When I need to store test certs on disk, then the strategy I've used is to have a script that generates all the certs from scratch, and store that in git alongside the generated certs. That way, I have a guaranteed-accurate record of what my certs actually are, and whenever there's some issue with the certs, I can fix the script and regenerate everything. It's way easier and more reliable than trying to figure out how to incrementally get from one state to another.

Now for urllib3... there's this additional complication that they have a whole bunch of certs and no-one knows what they actually are. It'd probably be a good idea to move them all over to the model I describe above, and as a bonus would help us figure out what features are missing in trustme [1]... but I can see why you might not want to bite off doing that all in one step :-). (Or maybe it would just take a tedious hour and you'd be done, idk.)

For urllib3, what do you think of creating a new cert directory that works the way I described, and for now just moving over the intermediate certs, with the plan of incrementally moving over the rest in the future?

[1] The two things I noticed in a quick skim: putting email addresses in the SAN field, and creating certs that use the legacy CN field instead of the SAN field. Both should be pretty easy to add I think. (Certainly they'd be easier than intermediate CAs!)
